title: Topic 198: Why Players Attend Esports Events: The Live Experience
content:
# Topic 198: Why Players Attend Esports Events: The Live Experience

## ABSTRACT

This paper embarks on a perilous journey into the heart of competitive gaming, a realm where the glow of a thousand screens illuminates the faces of the faithful. We seek to answer a question that has baffled sociologists, economists, and concerned parents for years: Why on earth do people leave the comfort of their perfectly calibrated gaming chairs to watch other people play video games in a crowded, noisy arena? This study delves into the psychological and social phenomena that compel a digital generation to seek an analog experience. Through a rigorous, albeit occasionally satirical, analysis of existing literature, real-world case studies, and a dash of armchair psychology, we posit that the live esports experience is a complex cocktail of community, catharsis, and the primal human need to shout “LEEEEROY JENKINS!” in a socially acceptable context. Our findings suggest that the allure of live esports is not merely about the games themselves, but about the shared, embodied experience of collective effervescence, a concept we’ve borrowed from Durkheim and applied to a world he could have never imagined, a world with significantly more dragons and space marines.

## INTRODUCTION

In the not-so-distant past, the term “gamer” conjured images of a solitary creature, hunched over a glowing screen in a dimly lit basement, fueled by a diet of lukewarm soda and questionable snack foods. This archetypal gamer was a digital hermit, a master of virtual worlds but a stranger to the sun. Yet, in a plot twist worthy of a blockbuster video game, these basement dwellers have emerged, blinking, into the daylight, and are now congregating in the thousands in stadiums and arenas around the world. The global esports market, a behemoth of an industry, is projected to reach staggering new heights, with a significant portion of its revenue generated from live events [1]. This mass exodus from the digital to the physical realm presents a fascinating paradox. Why would a generation that has perfected the art of online interaction, a generation that can order a pizza, find a soulmate, and attend a university lecture without ever leaving their house, choose to attend a live esports event?

This paper will argue that the answer lies in the very human need for what we are calling “embodied physicality” in a disembodied, digital world. We will explore the psychological drivers that push fans to trade their high-speed internet connections for overpriced hot dogs and the chance to sit in a slightly uncomfortable plastic chair for eight hours. We will examine the social dynamics of these events, from the collective roar of the crowd to the shared agony of a favorite team’s defeat. We will also, with a healthy dose of academic rigor and a slightly less healthy dose of sarcasm, dissect the existing literature on the subject, much of which seems to have been written by people who think “Fortnite” is a brand of camping equipment.

Our investigation will take us from the hallowed halls of the League of Legends World Championship to the raucous arenas of The International. We will analyze the strange and wonderful rituals of esports fandom, from the intricate cosplay to the surprisingly wholesome fan-made signs. We will even dare to ask the most profound question of all: is it really about the game, or is it about finding a place where you belong, a tribe of fellow travelers in the vast and often bewildering landscape of the 21st century? So, grab your oversized foam finger, your novelty hat, and your a sense of humor, because we are about to dive headfirst into the glorious, chaotic, and surprisingly profound world of live esports events.

## LITERATURE REVIEW

The academic world, in its infinite wisdom, has recently turned its gaze towards the burgeoning phenomenon of esports. Scholars, armed with their trusty toolkits of theories and methodologies, have begun to dissect the esports spectator with the same fervor they once reserved for Shakespearean sonnets and the mating habits of the fruit fly. The resulting body of literature is a fascinating, if at times bewildering, tapestry of insights, jargon, and the occasional, glaringly obvious conclusion.

A significant portion of the research, for instance, has been dedicated to proving that people who attend live esports events are, in fact, motivated by a desire to be there. A groundbreaking study by Pu, Xiao, and Kota (2021) bravely posits that motives for attending live esports events are, and I quote, “a necessary step in the continued understanding of esports” [2]. This stunning revelation, which surely sent shockwaves through the academic community, is a testament to the kind of bold, incisive analysis that we have come to expect from the field. The authors go on to develop a complex, eight-factor measurement scale to quantify these motivations, a tool that will no doubt be invaluable to future generations of scholars seeking to understand why people do things they want to do.

Another popular avenue of inquiry has been the application of the Stimulus-Organism-Response (S-O-R) model to the esports context. A particularly riveting paper by Cerqueira, Ribeiro, and de Almeida (2025) suggests that the “physical environment” of an esports event (the stimulus) can influence the “affective responses” of fans (the organism), which in turn affects their “behavioural intentions” (the response) [1]. In other words, if the lights are flashy and the music is loud, people might have a good time and want to come back. This is a truly revolutionary idea, and one that will surely change the way we think about live events forever. The authors, to their credit, do acknowledge the “hybridized” nature of esports events, a space where the digital and the physical collide in a glorious explosion of pixels and pyrotechnics. They also touch upon the unique culture of esports fandom, including the phenomenon of cosplay, where fans dress up as their favorite characters, a practice that is, as of yet, sadly under-researched in the context of traditional sports. Imagine, for a moment, a grown man in a full-body Pikachu costume at a baseball game. The possibilities are endless.

Of course, no academic discussion of esports would be complete without a nod to the gender divide. The aforementioned study by Pu, Xiao, and Kota (2021) found that male attendees are more likely to be driven by “knowledge acquisition, game drama, and social interaction” than their female counterparts [2]. This suggests that men, in their infinite quest for self-improvement, attend esports events to learn, to experience the thrill of competition, and to make friends. Women, presumably, are just there for the overpriced nachos. This is, of course, a gross oversimplification, but it does highlight the tendency of some research to fall back on tired gender stereotypes. It also provides an excellent opportunity for some light-hearted mockery, which is, after all, the highest form of academic discourse.

In conclusion, the existing literature on esports event attendance is a rich and varied field, full of fascinating insights and the occasional, unintentional comedic gem. While some of the research may state the obvious with an almost poetic sense of gravity, it also provides a solid foundation for further inquiry. The concepts of “embodied physicality,” the S-O-R model, and the unique cultural practices of esports fandom are all valuable tools for understanding this strange and wonderful new world. And, if nothing else, the research has given us a whole new vocabulary of jargon to deploy at cocktail parties. So, the next time someone asks you why you’re going to a video game tournament, you can confidently tell them that you’re seeking a “mutually constituted experience of virtual and physical space.” They’ll be so impressed, they won’t even have time to ask you what that means.

## ANALYSIS

To truly understand the magnetic pull of a live esports event, we must venture beyond the sterile confines of academic theory and into the messy, exhilarating reality of the human psyche. We must, in essence, put on our lab coats, but also our novelty foam fingers. The decision to attend a live esports event is not a simple one; it is a complex tapestry woven from threads of psychological need, social desire, and a deep-seated love for watching pixels explode in glorious, high-definition fashion.

At the heart of this phenomenon lies the concept of **“embodied physicality,”** a term we’ve borrowed from the hallowed halls of academia and repurposed for our own nefarious, comedic ends [2]. In an increasingly digital world, where our lives are mediated by screens and our social interactions are often reduced to a series of clicks and likes, the live esports event offers a powerful antidote: a real, tangible, and often sweaty, experience. It is a chance to be physically present, to feel the rumble of the bass in your chest, to high-five a complete stranger, and to participate in a collective, primal scream of joy or despair. This is the “O” in the S-O-R model, the organism’s internal, affective response to the stimulus of the live event [1]. The flashing lights, the roaring crowd, the palpable tension in the air – these are not mere distractions; they are the very ingredients of a powerful emotional cocktail.

Let us consider, for a moment, the humble **“wave.”** This seemingly simple act of collective choreography, a staple of traditional sporting events, takes on a new and profound meaning in the context of esports. In a stadium filled with people who spend a significant portion of their lives in virtual worlds, the wave is a powerful symbol of shared physical presence. It is a declaration that “we are here, together, in this space.” It is a moment of pure, unadulterated, and slightly dorky, community. This is the social dimension of the live experience, a factor that the research tells us is a particularly strong motivator for male attendees, who are, apparently, on a noble quest for friendship and camaraderie [2].

But the live esports experience is not just about community; it is also about **catharsis**. The drama of a hard-fought match, the thrill of a last-minute victory, the agony of a crushing defeat – these are not just games; they are epic narratives, played out in real-time. And for the fans in the stands, these narratives provide a powerful emotional release. The collective gasp as a player makes a risky move, the roar of approval as a team pulls off an impossible comeback, the shared groan of disappointment as a favorite is eliminated – these are the sounds of catharsis. It is a chance to experience a full spectrum of human emotion in a safe and controlled environment, a world where the stakes are high, but the consequences are, for the most part, confined to the virtual realm.

And then, of course, there is the **knowledge acquisition** factor, another key motivator for our male attendees [2]. The live event is a chance to learn from the best, to see the pros in action, and to pick up new strategies and techniques. It is a pilgrimage to the holy land of high-level play, a chance to sit at the feet of the masters and absorb their wisdom. Or, more accurately, it is a chance to yell at the screen and second-guess every move they make, because, as every gamer knows, we are all experts in hindsight.

In conclusion, the analysis of the live esports experience reveals a complex interplay of psychological and social factors. It is a quest for embodied physicality in a disembodied world, a search for community in an age of digital isolation, a need for catharsis in a world that often feels emotionally sterile, and a thirst for knowledge in a world that is constantly changing. It is, in short, a very human experience, albeit one with a lot more dragons and explosions than your average Tuesday.

## REAL-WORLD EXAMPLES

Theory is all well and good, but to truly grasp the glorious absurdity of the live esports experience, we must turn to the real world, to the stories and anecdotes of the fans who have braved the crowds, the overpriced concessions, and the occasional, unfortunate body odor to witness their favorite games played live.

Consider the case of the **2018 League of Legends World Championship in Seoul**. One intrepid fan, in a now-legendary Reddit post, described the experience as “like a rock concert, but with more nerds” [3]. This eloquent summary captures the essence of the live esports event: the energy and excitement of a major cultural gathering, combined with the unique and often quirky culture of the gaming community. The fan goes on to describe the roar of the crowd as the home team, Gen.G, took the stage, a sound he compares to “a thousand dragons all waking up at once.” This is the embodied physicality we spoke of earlier, the raw, visceral power of a shared emotional experience.

Then there is the tale of the **“shoey,”** a bizarre and uniquely Australian ritual that has, for reasons that defy all logic, become a staple of CS:GO tournaments down under. For the uninitiated, a “shoey” involves drinking a beer out of a shoe. Yes, you read that correctly. At the Intel Extreme Masters Sydney, it is not uncommon to see fans, and even the occasional pro player, partaking in this strange and unsanitary tradition. This is a prime example of the unique and often baffling rituals that emerge within the esports community. It is a testament to the fact that, when you get enough gamers in one place, things are bound to get weird.

And who could forget the **“sad fan”** from The International 2017, the premier Dota 2 tournament? During a particularly tense match, the camera zoomed in on a fan in the crowd, his face a mask of pure, unadulterated despair as his favorite team, Evil Geniuses, was eliminated. The image quickly became a meme, a symbol of the emotional rollercoaster that is esports fandom. But it is also a powerful reminder of the genuine passion and emotional investment that fans have in these games. This is not just a hobby; it is a matter of the heart.

Finally, let us not overlook the simple, wholesome joy of the **fan-made sign**. At any given esports event, you will see a veritable sea of cardboard and sharpie, bearing messages that range from the supportive (“I believe in you!”) to the slightly unhinged (“Marry me, Faker!”). These signs are a testament to the creativity and humor of the esports community. They are a way for fans to feel like they are a part of the show, to communicate with their heroes, and to share a laugh with their fellow fans. They are, in their own small way, a work of art.

These are just a few of the countless stories and anecdotes that paint a picture of the live esports experience. It is a world of roaring crowds, bizarre rituals, and heartfelt emotions. It is a world where a grown man can drink a beer out of a shoe and be hailed as a hero. It is a world where a sad fan can become an internet legend. It is, in short, a world that is every bit as strange, wonderful, and human as the virtual worlds it celebrates.

## DISCUSSION

The journey into the heart of the live esports experience has been a long and winding one, filled with academic jargon, questionable humor, and a surprising number of references to grown men drinking out of shoes. But what does it all mean? What are the broader implications of this strange and wonderful phenomenon for our understanding of human behavior and consumer habits?

First and foremost, the rise of live esports events challenges our traditional notions of community and social interaction. In an age of digital atomization, where our social lives are increasingly lived online, the esports event offers a powerful counter-narrative. It is a reminder that, for all our technological advancements, we are still fundamentally social creatures who crave face-to-face interaction. The desire to be part of a tribe, to share a physical space with like-minded individuals, is a powerful one, and the esports event provides a ready-made community for a generation that has often been accused of being disconnected.

Furthermore, the live esports experience offers a fascinating case study in the blurring of the lines between the digital and the physical. The concept of “embodied physicality” is not just a clever turn of phrase; it is a genuine reflection of the way in which our virtual and real-world lives are becoming increasingly intertwined [2]. The esports event is a space where the digital is made manifest, where the pixels on the screen are translated into the roar of the crowd and the palpable tension in the air. This has profound implications for how we think about consumer experiences in the 21st century. It is no longer enough to simply provide a product or a service; brands must now create immersive, multi-sensory experiences that engage consumers on both a physical and an emotional level.

Finally, the live esports event is a testament to the power of passion. The fans who attend these events are not just casual observers; they are deeply invested in the games, the players, and the community. This passion is the engine that drives the entire esports industry, and it is a force that should not be underestimated. It is a reminder that, in a world of fleeting trends and disposable entertainment, there is still a place for genuine, heartfelt enthusiasm. And it is a reminder that, sometimes, the most profound human experiences can be found in the most unlikely of places: a crowded arena, a glowing screen, and a shared love for the beautiful, chaotic, and utterly ridiculous world of competitive gaming.

## CONCLUSION

In conclusion, our scholarly sojourn into the world of live esports events has revealed a truth that is both profound and profoundly simple: people go to these things because they are fun. They are a chance to be part of something bigger than yourself, to share a passion with thousands of other people, and to experience the thrill of competition in a way that is both visceral and deeply human. The academic theories, the psychological frameworks, the eight-factor measurement scales – these are all just fancy ways of saying that we are, at our core, social creatures who crave connection, excitement, and the occasional, socially-sanctioned opportunity to scream our heads off.

So, the next time you see a headline about a sold-out esports tournament, don’t be so quick to dismiss it as a fad or a passing trend. Remember the roar of the crowd, the passion of the fans, and the strange and wonderful rituals of the gaming community. And remember that, in a world that is often divided, there is something truly special about a place where people from all walks of life can come together to celebrate a shared love for something as simple, and as beautiful, as a well-played game.
references:
[1] Cerqueira, L., Ribeiro, T., & de Almeida, V. M. C. (2025). Hosting eSports events: the quality-response-behavioural intentions relationship of eSports fans. *Frontiers in Sports and Active Living*, *7*, 1547097. https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12183061/

[2] Pu, H., Xiao, S., & Kota, R. W. (2021). Virtual games meet physical playground: Exploring and measuring motivations for live esports event attendance. *Sport in Society*, 1-18. https://ecommons.udayton.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi?article=1109&context=hss_fac_pub

[3] Reddit. (2018). *What is the league world finals like in person?* https://www.reddit.com/r/leagueoflegends/comments/1esfqc7/what_is_the_league_world_finals_like_in_person/
